text.title: gce-autoscaling Failure Modes
text.summary: Migrated antipatterns for gce-autoscaling as failure modes. Add detector + repair clauses where missing.

failure-mode.description: Setting the autoscaler cooldown period shorter than the VM startup time.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Starting VMs consume high CPU during initialization. A short cooldown causes the autoscaler to observe high CPU across the fleet and issue additional scale-out before the in-progress scale-out has had time to reduce the average CPU. This produces over-provisioning proportional to (startup_time / cooldown_period).

failure-mode.description: Using only CPU utilization as the autoscaling signal for an HTTP(S) frontend behind a load balancer.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: CPU does not accurately reflect request rate for I/O-bound or network-bound services. A service can be CPU-idle while being request-saturated. Use load_balancing_utilization or a custom RPS metric as the primary signal for HTTP frontends.

failure-mode.description: Setting target CPU utilization above 80% for production autoscaling.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: At 80-90% utilization there is no headroom for traffic spikes. By the time the autoscaler decides to scale out (after observing sustained high CPU), latency has already spiked. Target 60% to preserve 40% spike headroom; this is the documented GCP recommendation.
